title: Moiras Nudes

jokes.text: Johnny enthusiastically explains the 'good timing' of them stepping out simultaneously, insisting the unremarkable coincidence is remarkable, while the other person grows increasingly confused and annoyed
jokes.setup: Two people step outside at the same time — a completely mundane coincidence
jokes.punchline: Johnny treats it as a profound, exciting event and cannot stop explaining it even when he admits 'no explanation is needed,' ultimately undermining his own point
jokes.explanation: A solid opener that establishes Johnny's endearing, slightly oblivious enthusiasm. The joke is that he can't stop explaining an observation that needs no explaining — and explicitly acknowledges this — yet keeps going. Good character work but runs slightly long for its payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Did you see that? We exited at the exact same time! What are the odds? This is incredible. This is fate. This is the most extraordinary thing that's ever happened to me.

jokes.text: David reveals his employer bought him a car (rental) and Johnny and Moira immediately pivot to 'Thank God we have a car again!' — David's withering 'Um, very unclear on the "we" part of that sentence'
jokes.setup: David casually mentions his employer arranged a car for him to get to work more efficiently
jokes.punchline: The family immediately assumes it's a family car; David shoots them down with devastating precision
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. The Roses' immediate entitlement impulse — assuming David's work perk is communal property — and David's clipped, surgical correction ('very unclear on the "we"') is quintessential character comedy for both parties. The line is highly quotable and captures the family dynamic efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that... David? In a car? David, you're driving?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis, I'm driving. It's a car. I operate it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know what a car is. I'm just shocked that you know how to drive one.

jokes.text: Moira notices a missing button on Johnny's jacket and says 'Oh ew, you poor thing' — treating a minor wardrobe flaw as evidence of genuine destitution
jokes.setup: Johnny is genuinely emotionally moved by seeing David with a car, revealing cracks in the family's financial facade
jokes.punchline: Moira's attention laser-focuses on a missing button as the most salient sign of their decline, with aristocratic horror
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Moira's priorities are so perfectly skewed — the family's financial collapse is processed through the lens of couture. 'Oh ew, you poor thing' is a funny line that only Moira would say, but it's a quick beat rather than a fully developed joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't get a nice car. I got a car. It's functional. It has wheels and an engine, and it does what a car is supposed to do.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it's a very nice car, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not nice! It's beige! It's aggressively beige. And the interior smells like a wet dog that's been left in the sun.

jokes.text: Johnny casually asks to add turkey sausages to the tab at the diner, and Twyla reveals they've hit their tab limit
jokes.setup: Johnny confidently orders and asks to put it on the tab as if this is routine
jokes.punchline: Twyla reveals the café has a limit on how much you can charge 'without actually making any effort to pay' — a politely devastating indictment
jokes.explanation: Twyla's phrasing — 'without actually making any effort to pay' — is the gem here. It's blunt in its honesty while remaining polite, which is perfectly in character for Twyla. The cringe of Johnny having to face this publicly is well-earned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can I borrow your car?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not my car, Alexis. It's the family car.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, so I can borrow it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. It's the family car, which means it belongs to all of us, which means it belongs to none of us.

jokes.text: Johnny deflects Alexis's concern by saying he just needs to 'make things liquid,' and Alexis immediately calls it out as a financial red flag from dating Wall Street losers
jokes.setup: Johnny uses corporate finance jargon to minimize his money problems
jokes.punchline: Alexis's experience dating wealthy Wall Street types has made her fluent in financial euphemism — she sees right through it
jokes.explanation: Excellent character integration — Alexis's backstory of dating wealthy men is used as the source of genuinely useful knowledge, which subverts her airhead persona in a satisfying way. 'Wall Street losers' is a perfect Alexis descriptor.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, I have to say, you're looking a little... rough around the edges these days.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, Alexis, when you're rebuilding a business empire from the ground up, grooming takes a backseat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I get that, but you look like you're about to ask me for spare change.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's actually not far off from where we started, so I'll take it as progress.

jokes.text: Johnny tells Alexis not to tell David or Moira because 'they're too fragile to handle this'
jokes.setup: Johnny is confiding in Alexis about financial trouble and asks her to keep it secret
jokes.punchline: He describes Moira — a grown woman — and David as 'too fragile,' which is both absurd and oddly accurate given the show's characterizations
jokes.explanation: Works as character comedy because it's both accurate (Moira and David are high-maintenance) and richly ironic (Alexis herself was previously considered the flaky one). A functional joke but not especially crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Could you just add that to our tab?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Of course, Mr. Rose. Although, I should mention your tab is now at $4,000.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, we'll pay it eventually.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm sure you will. In about... never.

jokes.text: Alexis tells Johnny 'You might wanna tell that to your face' after he insists there's nothing to worry about
jokes.setup: Johnny insists everything is fine and no one needs to worry
jokes.punchline: Alexis points out his face is visibly betraying his anxiety — a classic 'your face tells a different story' beat delivered with Alexis's characteristic breezy precision
jokes.explanation: Short, sharp, perfectly landed. 'You might wanna tell that to your face' is economically brilliant — maximum information per word, perfectly timed as a scene-closer. Alexis delivering something this observationally smart is part of her character's slow reveal as more perceptive than she appears.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to start thinking about our liquid assets and diversifying our portfolio.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, finally! I've been diversifying my portfolio since I was sixteen.

jokes.text: Moira asks Stevie to find her nude photos online because 'I can't type, and I don't care to relive my days as a secretary'
jokes.setup: Moira needs someone to search for embarrassing photos of her and enlists Stevie
jokes.punchline: Her refusal to type is framed not as incompetence but as a matter of former professional dignity
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira: the refusal to type is presented as a regal boundary rather than an admission of helplessness. Her class-based aversion to clerical work is perfectly in character and funny because it's completely sincere.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, we've lost everything. Our entire fortune is gone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Not everything, darling. I still have my wigs.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, Moira. Your wigs. The $47,000 collection of wigs.

jokes.text: Stevie warns Moira: 'just give me a minute, because we're only one small step above dial-up here'
jokes.setup: Moira is impatient to find her nude photos online
jokes.punchline: Stevie deflates her urgency with a sardonic admission about the motel's internet quality
jokes.explanation: Functional Stevie deadpan. The 'one small step above dial-up' line grounds the absurdity of the scene with Schitt's Creek poverty humor. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I couldn't possibly. I don't know how to use a computer. I've never touched one in my life.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I've touched one, but not in a way that would be helpful to anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I once sat on one at a gala in Monaco. Does that count?

jokes.text: 'Okay, there's a bunch of you with O.J. Simpson. And you with Robert Blake. "The top eleven photographs of Moira Rose with future murder suspects."'
jokes.setup: Stevie is searching for Moira's nude photos online; instead she finds celebrity photos
jokes.punchline: The search results reveal Moira posed with multiple men who later became accused murderers — compiled into a listicle
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalation from 'O.J. Simpson' to 'Robert Blake' to the absurd specificity of 'top eleven' (not ten — eleven!) murder-suspect photos is excellently constructed. The dark comedy of Moira's glamorous past being catalogued this way is surprising and hilarious. The listicle framing is a sharp contemporary touch. Genuinely original.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why are you looking for compromising photos of my mother?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not looking for compromising photos. I'm just seeing what comes up when you search her name online.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, there's this headline: 'Local Woman's Suspicious Connections to Multiple Criminal Enterprises.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's just from her time in the entertainment industry.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Why were you in a paddle boat with Phil Spector?' Moira: 'And doesn't he look good? That was the one time he let me play with his hair.'
jokes.setup: The running revelation that Moira was photographed with future murder suspects continues with Phil Spector
jokes.punchline: Moira's response completely ignores the murder-suspect angle and focuses on the paddle boat, his hair, and a fond memory — entirely oblivious to the darkness
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback escalation. Moira's complete lack of awareness about the murder-suspect implications — replaced by warm nostalgia for Phil Spector's hair — is both darkly funny and completely in character. 'That was the one time he let me play with his hair' is an outstanding line: specific, absurd, and delivered with total sincerity. Her subsequent 'I shouldn't have to explain myself' makes it even better.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, Phil Spector. Yes, we met at a charity gala in the '80s. He was very charming, very talented. We danced, had cocktails. I had no idea about any of that other business. I just remember thinking he had wonderful hands for a murderer— I mean, for a musician.

jokes.text: Stevie's reaction when Moira reveals she did 'a series of very tasteful nudes with Richard Avedon in the 70s' — 'This is really not in my job description so...'
jokes.setup: Moira casually drops that she did nude photography with one of the most famous photographers in history
jokes.punchline: Stevie's only response is to distance herself from the task professionally — classic deadpan horror with perfect comic restraint
jokes.explanation: Stevie's reaction is perfectly calibrated. Rather than shock, she retreats into the language of professional boundaries — 'not in my job description' — which is funnier than any overt reaction would be. Classic Stevie deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, I've never had a pimple in my life. I think it's because I've always had really good skincare, but also just like, really good genes. My dermatologist in New York said I had the skin of a teenager, and I was like, 'Well, I am a teenager,' but he was like, 'No, like a really, really good-looking teenager.'

jokes.text: Moira: 'What, do you have some kind of childproof lock on this Internet?' Stevie: 'Nope, that would make my job very boring.'
jokes.setup: The nude photos aren't appearing in search results; Moira suspects censorship
jokes.punchline: Stevie's denial implies she regularly uses the motel internet for adult content — delivered with complete casualness
jokes.explanation: A quick, punchy exchange. Stevie's self-deprecating implication about her own internet habits is funny because it's delivered so matter-of-factly. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Did that just fall on the floor? This floor hasn't been cleaned since the Carter administration. I'm not putting that back on my face. I'd probably get some sort of flesh-eating disease.

jokes.text: Moira lectures David about washing his face with 'a bar of motel hand soap,' claiming she's always had clean, radiant skin but 'I'm an anomaly'
jokes.setup: David has a pimple and Moira sees it as a teachable moment
jokes.punchline: Moira frames her own perfect skin not as luck but as an anomaly — a humble-brag so audacious it loops back around to being funny
jokes.explanation: Moira's self-admiration delivered as scientific fact ('I'm an anomaly') is very in-character. A solid character beat that sets up the subsequent exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, that's from living in a motel room. You're breaking out from the stress of it all.

jokes.text: David insists his pimple is 'just my anxiety clogging up my pores.' Moira: 'And forming zits.'
jokes.setup: David refuses to call his blemish a zit, reframing it in quasi-medical anxiety language
jokes.punchline: Moira accepts his premise and completes it with clinical precision: yes, anxiety — and zits
jokes.explanation: A tight two-line joke. David's self-deception is punctured by Moira's perfectly timed addition. The comedy is in how she doesn't argue with him — she simply completes his sentence in a way that undermines it. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need help finding some nude photos of myself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would you need help finding photos of yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because they're not of me, they're of someone who looks like me, and I need to find them before they surface.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's like looking for a needle in a haystack, except the needle is my body and the haystack is the entire internet.

jokes.text: David is on the floor desperately salvaging something (skincare product implied) and Moira asks 'Wait, you're going to put that on your face after it's been on the floor?' David: 'I don't even wear good socks in here!'
jokes.setup: David is scrambling on the motel room floor in a panic
jokes.punchline: His justification for the floor-based face product is that he doesn't even dignify the floor with good socks — a deeply specific indicator of how low standards have fallen
jokes.explanation: David's sock hierarchy as a metric of self-respect is a wonderful detail. The joke works because of its specificity — not just 'I don't care about the floor' but 'I don't even wear GOOD socks in here.' That's rich character texture in a single line.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: There are nude photos of me online.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, there are nude photos of me online too, so...

jokes.text: Moira tells David they've 'arrived at an awkward moment in our parent/child relationship.' David: 'I'd argue that moment happens daily.'
jokes.setup: Moira is about to ask David to find nude photos of her and prefaces it with theatrical gravity
jokes.punchline: David immediately deflates her dramatic framing — the 'awkward moment' is perpetual, not special
jokes.explanation: A clean, satisfying exchange. David's weary resignation — 'that moment happens daily' — is perfectly in character and efficiently delivered. It also functions as meta-commentary on their entire relationship dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not looking for my mother's nude photos. I'm trying to find pictures of her in a context where she's fully clothed and making a sensible life choice.

jokes.text: "No, I tried to ask Stevie, but her computer is several years old, and apparently quite prudish."
jokes.setup: David tells Moira she can ask literally anyone else to search for her nude photos
jokes.punchline: Moira blames the failure to find her nudes on the computer being 'prudish' — anthropomorphizing technology with her characteristic baroque logic
jokes.explanation: Calling the computer 'prudish' is a genuinely original comic construction — only Moira would blame the technology's moral character for failing to find nude photos. High character integration and originality. This is the kind of absurdist logic that defines Moira Rose as a comedic creation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I think we've reached that awkward parent/child moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think we reached that about fifteen years ago, and we've just been living in it ever since.

jokes.text: Moira asks David to search for her nude photos online. David: 'That's a real quick no. Um, you can ask literally anyone else.'
jokes.setup: Moira has led up to asking David — her son — to find nude photos of her on the internet
jokes.punchline: David's reaction is immediate, categorical, and delivered with the flat exhaustion of someone who has seen too much already
jokes.explanation: 'A real quick no' is an excellent turn of phrase — it communicates the speed and totality of his refusal. The cringe premise (son searching for mom's nudes) is mined efficiently without dwelling too long.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, what are these search terms? 'Ethereal goddess in repose'? 'Transcendent form suspended in time'?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not going to search for 'naked pictures of me.' That's vulgar.

jokes.text: Moira: 'If someone has to find naked pictures of me, better you than a stranger in a storm cellar!'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to justify why David specifically should be the one to find her nudes
jokes.punchline: The alternative she conjures — a stranger in a storm cellar — is a bizarrely specific image that reveals she's thought about this in disturbing detail
jokes.explanation: A genuinely great line. 'A stranger in a storm cellar' is perfectly absurd — specific enough to be funny, dark enough to suggest a deeply weird mental image, and delivered by Moira with complete sincerity as though this is a reasonable argument. High quotability, strong originality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't understand. Where are the nudes? Every other semi-relevant public figure has at least one compromising photo circulating on the internet. Where are mine?

jokes.text: Alexis wanders in asking 'What's going on?' and is casually informed: 'There are nude photos of me all over the Internet.' Alexis: 'Um, are you sure you're not thinking of mine?'
jokes.setup: Alexis walks into the middle of the nude photo crisis
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately assumes the confusion might be her own nude photos — implying she also has nude photos circulating online, delivered with complete breezy nonchalance
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. Alexis's line does enormous character work in nine words: it confirms she has her own online nude history, she's completely unbothered by it, and she's casually helpful about it. The character integration is near-perfect — only Alexis could deliver this with exactly this energy. Highly quotable and a genuine laugh-out-loud moment.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: My uncle lost his arm in a workplace accident, and he couldn't afford the medical bills, so now he has a lot of debt.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: But at least he doesn't have to worry about paying for two sleeves anymore.

jokes.text: David on the search terms: 'Your name, and "nude." Three words I thought I'd only have to type if I was held at gunpoint.'
jokes.setup: David has been reluctantly searching for his mother's nude photos and explains his search terms
jokes.punchline: The gunpoint hypothetical — the only scenario extreme enough to justify this — is perfectly David: hyperbolic, self-pitying, and exactly right
jokes.explanation: An outstanding line. The specificity of 'held at gunpoint' as the threshold for this particular internet search is both funny and deeply in character for David's theatrical sense of personal violation. Strong structure, excellent economy, completely earned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, yes, I would love a smoothie. You know, when we were poor, we couldn't afford smoothies. We had to blend our own fruit, which sounds nice, but we didn't have a blender. So we just... mashed it with our hands. In a cup. It was basically just... sad juice.

jokes.text: Moira suggests search terms: 'Try "effervescence." No, I'm sorry, "small umbrella." No wait, "Siamese eels."'
jokes.setup: The obvious search terms have failed to locate the photos; Moira offers alternatives
jokes.punchline: Each suggested search term is more surreal than the last, implying a rich, completely opaque system of personal organization that only makes sense to Moira
jokes.explanation: Genuinely excellent. The escalating absurdity of Moira's suggested search terms — 'effervescence,' 'small umbrella,' 'Siamese eels' — implies an entire private mythology of self-categorization that we will never fully understand. Each term is more baffling than the last. This is character comedy at its finest: the joke could ONLY be Moira, and it raises more questions than it answers.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So I'd like to offer you a position at the firm. It would be part-time, flexible hours.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes! I would love a position. Like, what kind of position are we talking about? Because I'm very flexible, but I do have a bad back.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, I mean the job would have flexible hours. You'd be working in the office.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, okay, but like, would I have my own office? Because I need a space where I can really express myself, and honestly, fluorescent lighting is very aging.

jokes.text: Moira exasperatedly: 'Honestly! Can no one find nude photos of me on the Internet?!'
jokes.setup: After an exhaustive search, no nude photos of Moira can be located
jokes.punchline: Moira frames the absence of her nude photos as a collective failure — outraged that the internet has let her down by NOT containing her nudes
jokes.explanation: A perfect scene-closing line. The entire premise is inverted: rather than being relieved her nudes aren't online, Moira is affronted. The line encapsulates her narcissism and self-obsession with perfect economy. Strong quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have to get rides from my own son.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, yes. That's what happens when you don't have a driver's license or a car.

jokes.text: David's reaction beat after the Siamese eels search turns up nothing — his stunned 'Oh... okay, no. There was nothing. I don't see anything.'
jokes.setup: Moira has suggested 'Siamese eels' as a search term for her nude photos
jokes.punchline: David's dazed report of no results — the flat delivery suggesting he's processing what he just had to type
jokes.explanation: A good reaction beat that lands on the absurdity of the previous joke. David's dazed non-response is the right button for the Siamese eels escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I remember driving you to all your lessons when you were a kid.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was 11.

jokes.text: Twyla tells Alexis about her Uncle Ken, who only has three fingers now because of debt — 'which is too bad, because he's deaf, and he only speaks using sign language.' Then: 'He made his choices.'
jokes.setup: Twyla is trying to be sympathetic to Alexis about financial trouble by sharing a relatable personal story
jokes.punchline: The story escalates into genuinely grim territory (debt collectors taking fingers from a deaf man), then is punctuated by Twyla's philosophical shrug: 'He made his choices'
jokes.explanation: Twyla's dark backstory drops are a signature of the show, and this is one of the best. The layering is exceptional: debt → mob-style finger removal → deaf man → sign language → 'he made his choices.' Each beat lands. Twyla's cheerful resignation at the end is perfectly in character and perfectly dark. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not giving you money. Do you know how many times I've had to Google myself to make sure there aren't any nude photos of me on the internet?

jokes.text: Patrick tells Alexis 'The amount of times that I run into you in this place is like, out of a romantic comedy'
jokes.setup: Patrick runs into Alexis at the diner
jokes.punchline: He frames their repeated encounters in romantic comedy terms — which is either sincere or awkward flirtation that lands strangely
jokes.explanation: A mild joke that works more as a character/relationship beat than pure comedy. The line is pleasant but not particularly sharp or surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Come here, let me give you a hug.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, we're in a car.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So? I can hug my son in a car.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's a seatbelt, and a steering wheel, and—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't care. Come here.

jokes.text: Alexis says she has a pimple because she washed her face with 'dirty hand soap.' Patrick: 'Yeah, just don't look at it.' Alexis: 'Thanks.'
jokes.setup: Alexis mentions her skin problem as a casual complaint
jokes.punchline: Patrick's solution — 'just don't look at it' — is so spectacularly unhelpful it becomes funny; Alexis's genuine 'Thanks' suggests she accepts this as useful advice
jokes.explanation: A quick, economical exchange. The joke is in the uselessness of the advice and Alexis's earnest acceptance of it. Not deep, but clean.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Do you have any typing skills?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, I text all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's not really the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I use my thumbs, so technically I'm typing with twice as many fingers as most people.

jokes.text: Alexis accidentally blurts that 'my entire family is broke and we can't afford to eat right now' while trying to explain she can't pay Patrick back for a smoothie
jokes.setup: Alexis is declining a free smoothie so she won't have to repay the debt
jokes.punchline: Her self-censorship fails catastrophically — the full family financial situation tumbles out in one embarrassing rush to a near-stranger
jokes.explanation: A good cringe beat. Alexis's lack of filter — confiding total family bankruptcy to someone she barely knows while trying to be politely dismissive — is both funny and revealing of her character.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: So tell me about a time you demonstrated leadership.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, well, I was definitely a trendsetter. Like, I was the first person in my friend group to get a certain designer bag, and then everyone else got it. So I guess you could say I'm a leader?

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: That's... not really what we mean by leadership.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, as I told InStyle magazine in 2009, 'I go where fashion takes me.' And people really do follow my lead. So...

jokes.text: Alexis, offered a receptionist job at a vet clinic: 'That's so sweet, but I would be uncomfortable accepting free money.' Patrick explains she'd be working and paid. 'Oh!'
jokes.setup: Patrick offers Alexis a job at his vet clinic
jokes.punchline: Alexis initially mistakes a job for charity — she has no framework for the concept of earning money through labor — then is genuinely surprised to learn employment works this way
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. The joke reveals with surgical precision that Alexis's only framework for receiving money is either gifts or inheritance — the concept that you'd work for it is a genuine revelation. The 'Oh!' of discovery is perfectly landed. Very strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I found several photos of myself online that I don't remember taking. One of them describes me as 'an aging glamour-pusher with a tenuous grasp on reality.'

jokes.text: Johnny reflects that he 'never thought I'd be in this situation — getting a ride home from work by my son.' David's beat reaction: 'That's the situation you never thought you'd be in?'
jokes.setup: Johnny reflects on how much his life has changed while getting a lift from David
jokes.punchline: David's implied list of more obviously degrading situations (living in a motel, being broke, etc.) makes Johnny's chosen complaint absurdly specific and understated
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed two-line joke. Johnny's failure to identify the actual indignities of their situation — zeroing in on the car ride — while David gestures at the enormous iceberg below is very funny and very economical. The timing implied by David's pause makes it land harder.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Are these... nude photos of you?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Those are not nudes, Stevie. They are artistic renderings of the female form. And yes, they are me, but they have been heavily photoshopped. My waist has been narrowed, my décolletage has been enhanced, and my skin has been smoothed to an airbrushed perfection that would make a Barbie doll weep with envy.

jokes.text: Johnny begins listing things he did for David — harpsichord lessons — 'I would drive you.' David: 'I was seven.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is working up to asking David for money by cataloguing past paternal investment
jokes.punchline: David's 'I was seven' punctures the implied guilt trip — driving a seven-year-old to lessons is expected parental behavior, not a debt to be called in
jokes.explanation: Clean and efficient. David's flat two-word response destroys Johnny's entire rhetorical structure. The harpsichord detail is a nice absurd touch — only a Rose child would have harpsichord lessons.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I simply took a photo of myself, and used a photo editing application to enhance my features.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you catfished yourself?

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Nothing, nothing, I'm having a little chat with my son, that's all, do I have to pay for that?' David, deadpan: 'Last I heard, you didn't have to pay...'
jokes.setup: Johnny is being evasive about why he's reviewing David's past investments in casual conversation
jokes.punchline: His defensive 'do I have to pay for that?' backfires when David — knowing Johnny is currently broke — applies it literally
jokes.explanation: A decent callback to the financial theme. David's literal application of Johnny's rhetorical question is a good beat, though not as sharp as some of the episode's other exchanges.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is depressing. I've searched through decades of photographs and I haven't found a single one where I look even remotely presentable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you know what this means? I'm going to have to use a photo from the '80s. The '80s, David!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I think... I think I'm experiencing regret for the first time in my life.

jokes.text: Johnny tells David this is 'so awkward for a father to come to a son for money.' David: 'Well, believe it or not, this is actually not the most awkward parent/son request I've gotten today.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is embarrassed to ask David for money
jokes.punchline: David references Moira's nude photo search request without naming it — the callback lands perfectly because the audience knows what he's referring to
jokes.explanation: A well-earned callback. The line works on multiple levels: it reassures Johnny (his request isn't that bad), it reveals David's ongoing trauma from Moira's request, and it connects the two storylines elegantly. The restraint of NOT naming Moira's request makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I had the most exquisite décolletage in those photos. And my derrière was positively Rubenesque. The lighting really accentuated my... how do I put this delicately... my feminine landscape.

jokes.text: Johnny tries to hug David in the car without undoing his seatbelt, resulting in an awkward failed hug. 'Well, I was trying to give you a hug.' David: 'I-I get the gesture.' Johnny: 'I should've undone the seatbelt.'
jokes.setup: David agrees to give Johnny money; Johnny is moved and attempts physical affection
jokes.punchline: The physical attempt fails due to the seatbelt; both acknowledge the failure and agree to accept the gesture instead. The physical comedy is compounded by the emotional awkwardness of Roses showing affection
jokes.explanation: A warm, funny moment that's about more than the physical comedy — the Roses are constitutionally incapable of genuine physical affection even when they want to have it, and the seatbelt becomes a perfect metaphor. The negotiation to 'just leave it at a gesture' and the eventual high five is a great comedic and emotional beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I've learned that life is about embracing your flaws. I mean, look at me — I've had more failed business ventures than successful ones, my marriages have a higher turnover rate than a fast-casual restaurant, and my wigs alone could populate a small country. But do I let that stop me? Well, yes, frequently. But the point is, I'm still here, still fabulous, still wearing last season's Valentino like it's couture.

jokes.text: The aborted hug resolved: 'High five, son.' David: 'Uh well, okay, okay.' Johnny: 'All right, okay.'
jokes.setup: A hug has been deemed impossible; they need an alternative expression of affection
jokes.punchline: A high five — the least emotionally resonant physical gesture — is settled upon as the Rose family's version of a tender father-son moment
jokes.explanation: The high five as substitute for genuine affection is a perfect Schitt's Creek beat — warm, funny, and revealing. The hesitant 'uh well, okay, okay' from David and the mutual 'all right, okay' perfectly captures how far this family still has to go emotionally.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The implication that children searching for their parents' nude photos is normal

jokes.text: Alexis insists on formally interviewing for the job Ted has already offered her, despite him pointing out she already has it
jokes.setup: Ted has already offered Alexis the job; she insists on going through a proper interview anyway
jokes.punchline: The insistence is completely unnecessary and slightly ridiculous, but also a genuine character moment — Alexis wants to earn it legitimately, even if the outcome is predetermined
jokes.explanation: A comedic premise more than a joke — the humor comes from the situation and what it reveals about Alexis's character growth. It sets up the better jokes in the interview scene.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I can't get any money out of this thing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not a 'thing,' it's an automated teller machine.

jokes.text: Ted conducting a formal job interview he already knows the outcome of — the theatrical 'ahem' and papers shuffling
jokes.setup: Alexis has demanded a real interview; Ted is now obligated to perform one despite having already given her the job
jokes.punchline: Ted gamely goes along with the charade, complete with formal mannerisms, while the absurdity of the situation is clear to both of them
jokes.explanation: A nice extended bit — the absurdity of both parties playing along with a pointless formality. Works as situational comedy but the individual beats aren't particularly sharp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you. Thank you so much for backing away. That really helped. I feel so much better now knowing that you're standing three feet away from me instead of four feet away.

jokes.text: Ted asks if Alexis has receptionist experience. 'That would be a no, but I have dealt with a lot of receptionists before, and so I feel like I have the right temperament for it.'
jokes.setup: Ted asks a standard interview question about prior relevant experience
jokes.punchline: Alexis's logic — that being a client who has dealt with receptionists qualifies her to be one — is hilariously backward, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A gem of Alexis logic. Her reasoning is impeccable from her point of view and completely absurd from everyone else's. The 'right temperament' conclusion is the cherry on top — she's deduced character traits from being on the consumer end. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, yes, it's broken. But more importantly, your sign says 'ATM Machine.' That's redundant. It's either an ATM or an automated teller machine. You don't say 'ATM Machine' any more than you'd say 'PIN number' or 'LCD display.' It's excessive and frankly, it's lazy.

jokes.text: Alexis shuts down the interview: 'I'm gonna stop you right there. I'm loving this Q and A, but I think what's important right now is my positive attitude. And I don't wanna brag, but "Us Weekly" once described me as "up for anything."'
jokes.setup: Ted is about to ask about spreadsheet skills — a question Alexis clearly cannot answer
jokes.punchline: Alexis pivots from professional qualifications to celebrity magazine descriptors, offering a 'Us Weekly' quote as a character reference in a job interview
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Alexis citing 'Us Weekly' as a professional reference — and doing so with the pride of someone presenting a glowing letter of recommendation — is a perfect encapsulation of her character. The specificity of 'up for anything' as the quote is delicious: it implies both tabloid notoriety and a complete lack of professional relevance. High quotability, excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: I spoke with one of your tellers about this yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Teller
dialogue.line: No, you didn't.

jokes.text: After Ted hires her: Alexis: 'You're a really good actor.' Ted: 'Thanks.' Alexis: 'You're welcome.'
jokes.setup: Ted has performed the interview with professional seriousness despite it being predetermined
jokes.punchline: Alexis compliments his acting; Ted accepts; Alexis adds 'You're welcome' — receiving credit for the compliment she just gave
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean button on the interview scene. 'You're welcome' after complimenting someone is a perfect Alexis-ism: she's somehow making his gracious acceptance of her compliment about her generosity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We've lost everything. Our entire fortune. Gone. But we're not giving up. We're going to rebuild. From nothing. Which is actually... quite noble when you think about it. We're like pioneers. Except without the skills. Or the land. Or any idea what we're doing.

jokes.text: The 'nude' photo Stevie found is Moira's face from Sunrise Bay photoshopped onto 'what I can only assume is an Indonesian lady boy.' Stevie: 'I thought maybe you just had a tan.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has finally found something incriminating — Moira is excited until she sees it
jokes.punchline: It's a photoshopped composite of Moira's soap opera character's face on a stranger's body; Stevie's deadpan 'I thought maybe you just had a tan' is perfect comic restraint in the face of something very strange
jokes.explanation: A strong visual gag (even in transcript form, it's vivid). Stevie's 'I thought maybe you just had a tan' is perfectly calibrated deadpan — she considered accepting this as real before offering the observation. A funny payoff to the whole search sequence.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to need you to do something for me. And I'm going to need you to do it without asking questions, without hesitation, and without making me feel like I'm asking for something unreasonable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need you to process this transaction in a way that is... expedient. Because I have had a very difficult day, and I am not in the emotional space to deal with any sort of bureaucratic pushback.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You seem like a kind person. I can tell by the way you're looking at me with what I can only assume is pity. And I'm going to choose to believe that you are someone who helps people. So please, help me.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I would really rather not dive any deeper.' Moira: 'No, it's not necessary.' Stevie: 'Because I ended up in some really scary, dark places of the Internet that I would rather not revisit.'
jokes.setup: The search for Moira's nudes is concluded
jokes.punchline: Stevie's admission that she has been to 'really scary, dark places of the Internet' in the course of this search — delivered with flat trauma — is funnier than any description of what she found
jokes.explanation: A good understated joke. Stevie's experienced internet trauma for Moira's vanity, and the way she mentions it with quiet horror is a solid character beat. The implication is funnier than any specifics.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My PIN is 1-2-3-4.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Teller
dialogue.line: Sir, you shouldn't say that out loud.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why not? It's my PIN.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Teller
dialogue.line: Because now everyone in the bank knows it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's a good thing I have such a distinctive voice then.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I am suddenly overwhelmed with regret. It's a new feeling for me, and I don't find it at all pleasurable.'
jokes.setup: Moira realizes the photos are gone forever
jokes.punchline: Her description of regret as 'a new feeling' she doesn't find 'pleasurable' treats a universal human emotion as a foreign substance — as if regret is something other people experience
jokes.explanation: An excellent Moira line. The idea that regret is 'new' to her — implying a lifetime of consequence-free self-absorption — and that she 'doesn't find it pleasurable' (as if she expected to enjoy guilt) is wonderful character writing. Catherine O'Hara would deliver this perfectly. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, well if Ted's offering you a job, you should absolutely take it. A man who once thought a white fanny pack was appropriate business attire is really the gold standard for professional judgment.

jokes.text: Moira laments that she should have appreciated her 'firm round mammae and Callipygian ass while I had them.'
jokes.setup: Moira is mourning the lost nude photographs as the only record of her youthful body
jokes.punchline: She describes her former body in a mixture of clinical Latin ('mammae,' 'Callipygian') and frank appreciation — a very Moira combination of pretension and vanity
jokes.explanation: Only Moira would eulogize her own body using the Latin term 'Callipygian' (meaning having well-shaped buttocks) while being completely sincere. The Latinate vocabulary applied to frank vanity is a perfect Moira joke. High character integration and strong quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I went through a proper interview.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I didn't answer most of the questions.

jokes.text: Stevie, clearly uncomfortable: 'If you're talking about your body, uh... I think you still look amazing.'
jokes.setup: Moira has been lamenting her former body to Stevie, who is being forced to navigate this awkward territory
jokes.punchline: Stevie's compliment is clearly delivered out of social obligation rather than genuine assessment — the 'uh...' before it does all the comedy work
jokes.explanation: A decent reaction beat that depends heavily on performance. The hesitation before the compliment does the comedic work. Not a great joke on paper but functional in context.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Oh my God, oh my God! Do you know what this is? Do you have any idea what I'm holding right now?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A button?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Not just *a* button. This is the button from my Italian suit. The one I wore to the Gala in '94. I've been looking for this for twenty-three years!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you don't even own that suit anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's beside the point. This is a win, David. In a sea of losses, this is a win. I'm going to frame it.

jokes.text: Moira's advice: 'Take a thousand naked pictures of yourself now. You may currently think, "oh, I'm too spooky." Or, "nobody wants to see these tiny boobies."'
jokes.setup: Moira takes Stevie's compliment as an opportunity to give life advice about documenting your youth
jokes.punchline: The imagined objections she puts in Stevie's mouth — 'I'm too spooky,' 'nobody wants to see these tiny boobies' — are both absurdly specific and oddly perceptive observations about Stevie's self-image
jokes.explanation: A standout Moira speech. 'I'm too spooky' and 'nobody wants to see these tiny boobies' are phrases that could only come from Moira — the first is weirdly specific about Stevie's particular kind of awkwardness, and the second is delivered with such unself-conscious directness that it's simultaneously offensive and kind. These are deeply quotable lines.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I got you a card.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, sweetheart.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a condolence card, but I wrote 'financial support' on the inside.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... that's very sweet, but also deeply depressing.

jokes.text: Moira: 'But believe me, one day you will look at those photos, with much kinder eyes and say, "dear God, I was a beautiful thing!"' Stevie: 'Will I?'
jokes.setup: Moira delivers her passionate speech about future self-appreciation
jokes.punchline: Stevie's two-word response — 'Will I?' — is perfectly deflating; it's not sarcastic, it's genuine uncertainty about whether she'll ever feel beautiful
jokes.explanation: The economy here is extraordinary. 'Will I?' does three things simultaneously: it's funny (deflating Moira's grand speech), it's warm (Moira's sincerity is touching despite her delivery), and it's a genuine character moment for Stevie (her self-doubt is real). Two syllables of perfect writing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is this? There's nothing in here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a sympathy card.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A sympathy card? For what?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: For your financial situation.

jokes.text: Moira's addendum: 'And make sure you submit those photos to the Internet. Otherwise, your own children will go looking for them one day and, tragically, they won't be there.'
jokes.setup: Moira has given advice to photograph yourself; she adds a final instruction
jokes.punchline: The instruction to post nudes online is delivered as responsible parenting advice, framed as a tragedy if neglected — a complete inversion of normal parental wisdom
jokes.explanation: A brilliant inversion of the episode's premise. The whole plot has been about finding/removing Moira's nudes, but she concludes by advising Stevie to UPLOAD hers for her future children. The logic is internally consistent for Moira and completely inverted from normal parental advice. Excellent callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, he's your father. You have to hug him.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't have to do anything.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, what is wrong with you? He's been nothing but supportive of you and your business.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He literally bought you that store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm aware.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And he did it with love. Unlike my gift, which was clearly a mistake because it's a scarf from a store that no longer exists.

jokes.text: David asks how much Johnny needs; it escalates: 'A couple hundred, three... four...' — Johnny: '$560.' David: 'So my entire paycheck, then.'
jokes.setup: David has agreed to give Johnny money and asks the amount
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'couple hundred' gradually escalates to David's entire paycheck — and David names the exact correspondence, making the devastation precise
jokes.explanation: A well-structured escalation. The comedy is in how quickly 'a couple hundred' becomes $560 — the entire paycheck — and David's matter-of-fact confirmation of the proportionality. The cringe is earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's wonderful, Johnny. Though I do think you're overlooking the fact that I carried both of those children for nine months each, endured two very difficult deliveries, and have spent the better part of two decades trying to keep them from embarrassing themselves in public. So while I'm thrilled that you've noticed their accomplishments, I think we can all agree that my contribution has been somewhat more substantial.

jokes.text: The ATM eats Johnny's card after repeated failed PIN attempts. David: 'Well, press eject!' Johnny: 'There's no eject on this machine!' David: 'Well, what this? There's eject.' Johnny: 'That's "cancel," that doesn't do anything!'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to withdraw money but the ATM has swallowed his card after wrong PIN attempts
jokes.punchline: David's confident instruction to 'press eject' reveals he has no idea how ATMs work; their increasingly panicked button-pressing debate is pure situational comedy
jokes.explanation: Good situational escalation. The image of the Roses treating an ATM like a DVD player ('press eject') is funny and in character for people who've never had to deal with mundane financial mechanics before. Runs a bit long but lands.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at me. I was a vision. I need to post this immediately.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, didn't you just say you were concerned about your privacy online?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, but that was before I saw my body.

jokes.text: David corrects Johnny mid-ATM-crisis: 'It's actually just "pin," you don't have to say "pin number," that's redundant.' Johnny: 'Okay, okay, wow, are you doing this right now?!'
jokes.setup: Johnny is in a state of panic at the ATM, having had his card swallowed
jokes.punchline: David chooses this moment to deliver a grammar lesson about the redundancy of 'PIN number' (Personal Identification Number number)
jokes.explanation: A great character comedy beat. David's compulsion to correct pedantic language errors even in moments of genuine crisis is perfectly in character and deeply funny. The joke also has a smart premise (PIN number IS technically redundant). Johnny's exasperated response lands the punchline.

jokes.text: The ATM eats Johnny's card; they argue about whether 'eject' or 'cancel' or 'the green one' would retrieve it
jokes.setup: The ATM has eaten Johnny's card after too many failed PIN attempts
jokes.punchline: Johnny and David's panicked, contradictory button-pressing instructions — 'Press eject!' / 'There's no eject on this machine!' / 'That's cancel, that doesn't do anything!' — is perfectly observed tech-panic chaos
jokes.explanation: Functional panic comedy — the 'press eject!' instruction for an ATM is particularly good (who says that?). The escalation of bad advice is relatable. Not exceptional writing but well-performed situational comedy.

jokes.text: Bank teller retrieves the eaten card and asks if David used the proper PIN. David: 'Yeah, that's what I asked him.' Johnny: 'Of course I used the proper pin. I know my pin number, it's my birthday.' Teller: 'Happy Birthday!' Johnny: 'No, the pin number is my birthday.'
jokes.setup: The bank teller asks about the PIN; Johnny says it's his birthday
jokes.punchline: The teller reflexively wishes him happy birthday, not registering that 'it's my birthday' referred to the PIN. The miscommunication is brief and perfectly timed.
jokes.explanation: A classic miscommunication joke executed with excellent timing. The teller's 'Happy Birthday!' is the perfect automatic response and Johnny's deadpan correction ('No, the pin number is my birthday') closes the loop cleanly. Good structure.

jokes.text: David keeps noting Johnny says 'pin number' instead of 'pin'; the bank teller confirms: 'He keeps saying pin number.'
jokes.setup: David's earlier PIN number pedantry continues
jokes.punchline: The bank teller — a stranger — independently notices and agrees with David's grammar complaint, validating his pedantry in the worst possible moment
jokes.explanation: A good callback that extends and escalates the PIN number running gag. The bank teller's alignment with David against Johnny while they're all supposed to be solving a financial crisis is wonderfully absurd.

jokes.text: Teller reveals the branch only has two tellers — herself and Carol — after Johnny insists he spoke to someone on the phone who said he didn't need a temporary PIN. 'It was a while ago.' 'It says the account was opened last week.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to explain he was told by a teller he didn't need the temporary PIN
jokes.punchline: The bank has only two staff members, the account was opened last week, and his alibi evaporates — every escape route closes
jokes.explanation: A functional cringe-comedy beat as Johnny's story falls apart. The small bank with only two staff members is a nice Schitt's Creek small-town detail. Not particularly sharp but serves the scene.

jokes.text: The bank teller reveals there's only her and Carol working there — implying Johnny talked to Carol when he thought he'd gotten advice from a proper 'teller'
jokes.setup: Johnny insists a teller told him he didn't need a temporary PIN
jokes.punchline: "Oh, which teller? Because it's only me and Carol here." — Carol is clearly the other person, not a teller, deflating Johnny's certainty
jokes.explanation: A quiet joke that lands well — 'it's only me and Carol here' implies the whole bank is comically understaffed and that Johnny's confident 'a teller told me' collapses. Small but effective.

jokes.text: David explains to the teller that they need money because 'my family is in debt. So if you could help me help him, that would be a wonderful thing.'
jokes.setup: The bank process has stalled; David intervenes to explain the urgency
jokes.punchline: David, who prides himself on detachment, publicly announces family financial distress to a bank teller with theatrical helplessness — a callback to Alexis doing the same at the diner
jokes.explanation: A mild callback — both Rose children overshare family financial shame to service workers. Not as sharp as Alexis's version, but functional.

jokes.text: Johnny says his new PIN aloud — '7-2-8-3' — in front of the teller and David. Teller: 'Sir, you just said your pin out loud, so I suggest you pick another one.'
jokes.setup: Johnny finally gets to choose a new PIN, restoring account access
jokes.punchline: He immediately announces it publicly, negating the entire security purpose of a PIN
jokes.explanation: A solid situational joke. Johnny's incompetence with basic banking escalates from wrong PIN to announcing the new PIN publicly. The teller's patient correction is funny in its professional restraint.

jokes.text: Alexis talking head: 'I never saw myself having like, a normal job. But I think it's gonna be kinda nice to make money and stuff.'
jokes.setup: Alexis reflects on her new employment
jokes.punchline: 'Make money and stuff' — her casual, vague framing of basic financial independence as a novel and mildly interesting concept
jokes.explanation: A mild character beat. 'Make money and stuff' perfectly captures Alexis's breezy vagueness about adult concepts, though it's not a joke so much as a tone-setting moment.

jokes.text: David: 'You're working for your ex.' Alexis defends Ted; David: 'Ted also proposed to you twice, so I'd say Ted's decision making skills are a little suspect.'
jokes.setup: David questions whether taking a job with her ex is a good idea
jokes.punchline: David's argument against Ted's judgment is that he proposed to Alexis twice — which is simultaneously a burn on Alexis and on Ted's romantic choices
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical burn. David deploys Alexis's history against her in a way that's both logically sound and insulting. The 'proposed twice' detail is used as evidence of poor judgment rather than romantic devotion, which is a smart inversion.

jokes.text: Alexis defends her interview: 'I went through the same interview process as everybody else. He asked me several questions, most of which I answered.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is defending the legitimacy of her interview to David
jokes.punchline: 'Most of which I answered' — the qualifier that exposes how poorly the interview actually went, delivered with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Most of which I answered' is a perfectly devastating self-indictment delivered with absolute confidence. The qualifier 'most of' does all the work — it's honest enough to be damning but said with enough breeziness to suggest Alexis considers this a win. Excellent economy, strong character integration. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Johnny enters holding up a single button from his jacket, triumphant: 'Well kids, you'll never guess what I just found outside. My button! Things are looking up!'
jokes.setup: The family is gathered after a day of financial crisis and anxiety
jokes.punchline: Johnny's victory — finding his missing jacket button — is presented with the same energy as a major financial turnaround, against a backdrop of genuine poverty
jokes.explanation: A well-earned callback to the button noted earlier by Moira. 'Things are looking up!' applied to finding a button while the family is broke is a perfect piece of tragicomedy — Johnny's optimism is both touching and absurdly misplaced. The callback structure gives it extra weight.

jokes.text: Alexis gives Johnny a card: 'My deepest condolences? ... "I know things have been tough lately, but I'm here for you, and hope this helps. Love, Alexis."' Johnny: 'Was there supposed to be something in here?' Alexis: 'In where?' Johnny: 'In the card?'
jokes.setup: Alexis gives Johnny a meaningful-looking envelope as a gift; he opens it with expectation of money
jokes.punchline: It's just a sympathy card with no money inside — and Alexis had no idea cards are supposed to contain anything, because she thought the card itself was the support
jokes.explanation: A warm, funny, and genuinely touching joke. Alexis chose a sympathy card — which suggests she understands the emotional dimension of what Johnny is going through — but the execution reveals she's never had to give someone practical financial support before. 'In where?' is perfectly innocent. The joke works on both comedic and emotional levels.

jokes.text: Johnny reads the card and asks: "Was there supposed to be something in here?" / "In where?" / "In the card?" / "What do you mean?" / "Oh, it's just the card."
jokes.setup: Johnny has read Alexis's sympathy card and waits for the actual gift
jokes.punchline: Alexis is genuinely puzzled that he'd expect something inside — the card WAS the gift — and her innocent 'What do you mean?' treats this as a completely normal thing
jokes.explanation: A good extension of the sympathy card joke. Alexis's genuine incomprehension that the card might be expected to contain something is funny — she's been so sheltered that the concept of 'card + gift' apparently never registered. Well-played.

jokes.text: Johnny insists Alexis gave 'more than a cheap card' while David clearly gave actual money. Moira: 'Both my kids are generous.' David's reaction as he has just given his entire paycheck
jokes.setup: Johnny is diplomatically praising both kids for their contributions — Alexis's empty card and David's entire paycheck
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'both my kids are generous' treats these wildly unequal contributions as equivalent; the audience knows David just gave his whole paycheck
jokes.explanation: A functional irony beat — the gap between Alexis and David's contributions is obvious to the audience if not to Johnny and Moira. David's silent suffering lands better in performance than on the page.

jokes.text: Moira asks why Johnny is hugging the kids. Johnny's explanations: 'I found my button. / Alexis got a job.' Moira: 'Alexis got a job!' — genuine shock
jokes.setup: Moira wants to know what's going on with the uncharacteristically affectionate scene
jokes.punchline: The news of Alexis getting a job produces a reaction of genuine, barely concealed astonishment from Moira — a vote of no-confidence in her own daughter
jokes.explanation: A solid reaction beat. Moira's shock reads as both funny and mildly insulting to Alexis. The repetition ('Alexis got a job! / Yeah / Alexis got a job!') signals that she needs a moment to process this news.

jokes.text: Moira to Alexis: 'Wow Alexis, look at you! Just starting out on a shiny new future!' — followed by a lingering, slightly horrified silence as she processes
jokes.setup: Moira tries to be supportive about Alexis's new job
jokes.punchline: The phrasing 'shiny new future' is delivered with the faintest edge — as though Alexis is a child just learning to walk rather than a grown adult getting a job — then the scene lingers in slightly uncomfortable celebration
jokes.explanation: A mild joke that's more about tone than content. Moira's slightly condescending enthusiasm works in performance but isn't a strongly constructed joke on the page.

jokes.text: Johnny, reflecting: 'They say it's through our children that we stay young.' Moira: 'But I haven't seen much effort on their part.'
jokes.setup: Johnny offers a warm, clichéd observation about children keeping parents young
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately turns it into an indictment of the children's failure to actively maintain their parents' youth — as though this were a job requirement the kids are slacking on
jokes.explanation: An excellent Moira line. The twist is brilliant: she takes a sentimental observation about children and reframes it as a performance review they're failing. 'I haven't seen much effort on their part' implies the children are contractually obligated to keep her young. Pure Moira narcissism distilled into one clean, quotable sentence.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Why don't you look in my folio? Inside pocket.' Moira finds a photograph and exclaims 'Oh, you dirty dog!'
jokes.setup: Moira is lamenting the loss of her nude photographs; Johnny suggests she look in his folio
jokes.punchline: He's kept a photograph of her from Greece 1987 — a sweet, slightly naughty romantic gesture that pays off the episode's central theme
jokes.explanation: A warm, well-earned payoff. The whole episode has been about finding these lost photos, and Johnny has quietly held one all along. 'Oh, you dirty dog!' is a perfect Moira reaction — delighted, flirtatious, and slightly theatrical. Good misdirection: the folio suggested something professional; it contained something intimate.

jokes.text: Moira admires her 1987 photo: 'Oh, I loved those earrings. And that skin. And that hair.' Johnny: 'I'm no Avedon honey, but I would say you still look pretty spectacular.'
jokes.setup: Moira examines the old photograph Johnny has kept
jokes.punchline: Moira's priorities in appreciating her past self — earrings before skin, skin before hair — and Johnny's gentle self-deprecation about not being Richard Avedon are both character-true and warm
jokes.explanation: A gentle, warm closing joke. Moira appreciating her earrings first is perfectly in character. Johnny's 'I'm no Avedon' callback is a sweet and self-aware touch. More warm than funny, but it earns the smile.

jokes.text: Final line — Moira, having just shared a romantic moment: 'Now how do I get this on the Internet? John!'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira have shared a tender, intimate moment with the old photo
jokes.punchline: Moira's first thought is to upload it to the internet — taking the romantic resolution of the episode and immediately converting it into her original vanity mission
jokes.explanation: The perfect episode-closing joke. It's a callback to the entire episode's A-plot, it's delivered immediately after what should be a tender romantic beat, and it encapsulates Moira's character completely: narcissism and vanity win over every other emotion instantly. 'John!' — shouted after him — is the ideal button. This is the best-structured joke in the episode: it earns its laughs through everything that came before it and lands with perfect comic timing as the final word.

tmdb_overview: Moira is worried that racy photos from her past will jeopardize the campaign; Johnny asks for financial help; Alexis gets a job.
insight: Moira's vanity becomes the engine: 51 jokes in 42 minutes, mostly character-driven.
